steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Ticket #4891 — user says their static site at /home/user/www isn't accessible. They've got index.html and some assets in there, just need a basic http server on port 8080. Python's already installed, don't need anything fancy, just get it serving so they can hit localhost:8080 and see the page.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@3092504f9901:/home/user#

Analysis: I'm currently in /home/user directory as root. The task is to serve static files from /home/user/www on port 8080. I need to first check if the www directory exists and contains the expected files (index.html and assets), then start a simple Python HTTP server.
Plan: First, I'll list the contents of /home/user to confirm the www directory exists. Then I'll check what's inside the www directory to verify the static files are there. Finally, I'll start Python's built-in HTTP server on port 8080 to serve the content.

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Extra text detected before JSON object

New Terminal Output:

root@3092504f9901:/home/user# ls -la
total 12
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:25 .
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:25 ..
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:25 www
root@3092504f9901:/home/user# ls -la www/
total 16
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:25 .
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:25 ..
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:25 css
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  332 Apr 29 10:25 index.html
root@3092504f9901:/home/user#

steps.message:
Analysis: Good! The www directory exists and contains an index.html file and a css directory with assets. Now I need to start a Python HTTP server on port 8080 to serve these files. I'll change into the www directory and start the server.
Plan: I'll cd into the www directory and then start Python's built-in HTTP server on port 8080. I'll use 'python3 -m http.server 8080' which will serve the current directory. This will keep running in the foreground to serve the static site.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@3092504f9901:/home/user# cd www
root@3092504f9901:/home/user/www# python3 -m http.server 8080
Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8080 (http://0.0.0.0:8080/) ...

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! The Python HTTP server is now running successfully on port 8080. The server is serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0:8080, which means it's accessible on localhost:8080. The static site at /home/user/www is now accessible as requested.
Plan: The task is complete. The HTTP server is running and serving the static site on port 8080. Users can now access the site at localhost:8080 and see the index.html page along with the CSS assets.

Analysis: The Python HTTP server is running and serving the static site from /home/user/www on port 8080. The server is listening on 0.0.0.0:8080, which means it's accessible via localhost:8080. The site should now be viewable with the index.html and CSS assets available.
Plan: The task has been successfully completed. The static site is now being served on port 8080 as requested, and users can access it at localhost:8080.
